title: he Calling of the Apostles part 2
scripture_text: ‘’One of those days Jesus went out to a mountainside to pray, and spent the night praying to God. When morning came, he called his disciples to him and chose twelve of them, whom he also designated apostles: Simon (whom he named Peter), his brother Andrew, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Simon who was called the Zealot, Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.’’
devotional:
There are a number of lessons this passage can teach us.

1. This story is about Jesus embracing his humanity.

Luke tells us that he prayed to God all night. As a man in the midst of the storm, Jesus probably felt the need to bring the pain of his heart to God. Jesus was not some kind of superhuman who was impervious to criticism, ridicule or threat. He was not a dispassionate ‘Tin man,’ who could remain unmoved or untouched by the mistreatment of others. So as a man, in all his human vulnerability he prayed to God and in humility and submission he yielded himself to the purposes of God, despite great opposition.

2. Jesus made time and space to deal with his circumstances before God.

This is the only occasion in the bible were we read of an individual spending the whole night in prayer. Jesus was very intentional in how he responded to the growing opposition and criticism he was facing. I love the fact that Jesus allowed his mistreatment by others to matter, he didn’t pretend it didn’t matter, or it wasn’t an issue that he could ignore. Rather, it seems that the only way he chose to respond to the increasing opposition was through a prolonged period of prayer.

When Luke speaks about Jesus praying ‘all night’ he uses the word dianyktereuo. This is the only time we find this word in the bible. Outside the bible it was used in the world of medicine to describe a whole nights vigil.

It also had a military rather than religious associations. I think this gives us some insight as to how Jesus was praying, was this a Pre-Gethsemane experience? Could it be that on that night as he wrestled in prayer He found the resolve and courage to go forward in his call and ministry?

It was in the context of Jesus dealing spiritually with the threatening issues of his life that he got a clearer understanding about the way forward.

There will be times in all our live when the pressure of life will stop us from moving forward in the direction God may be calling us to, we may feel overwhelmed or paralysed with fear, and unfortunately we can allow the difficulties of the present to prevent us from stepping into the future.

3. This night of prayer may have been more about receiving the courage to lead, than about the wisdom to decide.

When Jesus called these disciples and chose them to be Apostles, we must remember that he had known them for a while. He had been in their homes, he had been at their places of work, their place of worship, he had partied with them, he had journeyed with them. But now it was time for change, a time to develop them from disciples to Apostles.

Before we go on, I think there is a lesson in this for us. As we deal with the conflicts and intimidations of life in a spiritual way, this will produce in us healing courage so we will be more equipped to lead and influence others for their good. We can allow unresolved issues of rejection, intimidation and misunderstanding to cause us to withdraw into cosy self protection from which we have no ability to be a positive influence or encouragement on/to others.

We have become so used to reading about Jesus and the Apostles that we have missed how radical Jesus is being here. Luke tells us at ‘daybreak’ he called together his disciples. I love that Luke refers to ‘daybreak’ it is as if he was saying, ‘this is a new day, Jesus has just spent the night wrestling in prayer and things are never going to be the same.

When Jesus called these disciples to become Apostles, he was making a very strong statement about who he was and how he saw his future. He was saying ‘I am much more than a Rabbi.’ The calling of the Apostles was a declaration of intent, it was a refusal to be intimidated, it was an expression of determination and certainty, it was the decree of a king.

The word for Apostles comes from Apostolos which refers to someone who was officially commissioned to a position or task, such as an envoy. Cargo ships were sometimes called apostolic, because they were dispatched with a specific shipment for a specific destination. In secular Greek, Apostolos was used of an admiral of a fleet sent out by the King on special assignment. In the ancient world, an Apostle was the personal representative of the King, functioning as an ambassador with the King’s authority and provided with credentials to prove he was the King's envoy.

You could say in Jesus’ world that Rabbi's had disciples and Kings had apostles. So when Jesus called them Apostles, it would have been very unexpected, it would have begun to change their understanding of Jesus. Their lives had now taken on a regal significance, they were now designated as emissaries of a King.
prayer: Father thank you that spending time with you in prayer can help us deal with difficult situations and give us courage to carry out your will for our lives.
html:
he Calling of the Apostles part 2

Luke 6:12-16 T
‘’One of those days Jesus went out to a mountainside to pray, and spent the night praying to God. When morning came, he called his disciples to him and chose twelve of them, whom he also designated apostles: Simon (whom he named Peter), his brother Andrew, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Simon who was called the Zealot, Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.’’

There are a number of lessons this passage can teach us.

1. This story is about Jesus embracing his humanity.

Luke tells us that he prayed to God all night. As a man in the midst of the storm, Jesus probably felt the need to bring the pain of his heart to God. Jesus was not some kind of superhuman who was impervious to criticism, ridicule or threat. He was not a dispassionate ‘Tin man,’ who could remain unmoved or untouched by the mistreatment of others. So as a man, in all his human vulnerability he prayed to God and in humility and submission he yielded himself to the purposes of God, despite great opposition.

2. Jesus made time and space to deal with his circumstances before God.

This is the only occasion in the bible were we read of an individual spending the whole night in prayer. Jesus was very intentional in how he responded to the growing opposition and criticism he was facing. I love the fact that Jesus allowed his mistreatment by others to matter, he didn’t pretend it didn’t matter, or it wasn’t an issue that he could ignore. Rather, it seems that the only way he chose to respond to the increasing opposition was through a prolonged period of prayer.

When Luke speaks about Jesus praying ‘all night’ he uses the word dianyktereuo. This is the only time we find this word in the bible. Outside the bible it was used in the world of medicine to describe a whole nights vigil.

It also had a military rather than religious associations. I think this gives us some insight as to how Jesus was praying, was this a Pre-Gethsemane experience? Could it be that on that night as he wrestled in prayer He found the resolve and courage to go forward in his call and ministry?

It was in the context of Jesus dealing spiritually with the threatening issues of his life that he got a clearer understanding about the way forward.

There will be times in all our live when the pressure of life will stop us from moving forward in the direction God may be calling us to, we may feel overwhelmed or paralysed with fear, and unfortunately we can allow the difficulties of the present to prevent us from stepping into the future.

3. This night of prayer may have been more about receiving the courage to lead, than about the wisdom to decide.

When Jesus called these disciples and chose them to be Apostles, we must remember that he had known them for a while. He had been in their homes, he had been at their places of work, their place of worship, he had partied with them, he had journeyed with them. But now it was time for change, a time to develop them from disciples to Apostles.

Before we go on, I think there is a lesson in this for us. As we deal with the conflicts and intimidations of life in a spiritual way, this will produce in us healing courage so we will be more equipped to lead and influence others for their good. We can allow unresolved issues of rejection, intimidation and misunderstanding to cause us to withdraw into cosy self protection from which we have no ability to be a positive influence or encouragement on/to others.

We have become so used to reading about Jesus and the Apostles that we have missed how radical Jesus is being here. Luke tells us at ‘daybreak’ he called together his disciples. I love that Luke refers to ‘daybreak’ it is as if he was saying, ‘this is a new day, Jesus has just spent the night wrestling in prayer and things are never going to be the same.

When Jesus called these disciples to become Apostles, he was making a very strong statement about who he was and how he saw his future. He was saying ‘I am much more than a Rabbi.’ The calling of the Apostles was a declaration of intent, it was a refusal to be intimidated, it was an expression of determination and certainty, it was the decree of a king.

The word for Apostles comes from Apostolos which refers to someone who was officially commissioned to a position or task, such as an envoy. Cargo ships were sometimes called apostolic, because they were dispatched with a specific shipment for a specific destination. In secular Greek, Apostolos was used of an admiral of a fleet sent out by the King on special assignment. In the ancient world, an Apostle was the personal representative of the King, functioning as an ambassador with the King’s authority and provided with credentials to prove he was the King's envoy.

You could say in Jesus’ world that Rabbi's had disciples and Kings had apostles. So when Jesus called them Apostles, it would have been very unexpected, it would have begun to change their understanding of Jesus. Their lives had now taken on a regal significance, they were now designated as emissaries of a King.

Prayer: Father thank you that spending time with you in prayer can help us deal with difficult situations and give us courage to carry out your will for our lives.
Bible in a year reading plan: -Joshua 15-17
